edge: The paper proposes a novel, abstraction-based, certified training method for robust image classifiers.
Via abstraction, all perturbed images are mapped into intervals before feeding into neural networks for training.
Via abstraction, all perturbed images are mapped into intervals before feeding into neural networks for training.
By training on intervals, all the perturbed images that are mapped to the same interval are classified as the same label.
By training on intervals, all the perturbed images that are mapped to the same interval are classified as the same label, rendering the variance of training sets to be small.
By training on intervals, all the perturbed images that are mapped to the same interval are classified as the same label, rendering the loss landscape of the models to be smooth.
Our training method enables a sound and complete black-box verification approach, which is orthogonal and scalable to arbitrary types of neural networks regardless of their sizes and architectures.
We evaluate our method on a wide range of benchmarks in different scales. The experimental results show that our method outperforms state of the art.
Consequently, our approach significantly improves the robustness of trained models.
The experimental results show that our method outperforms state of the art by reducing the verified errors of trained models up to 95.64%.
The experimental results show that our method outperforms state of the art by totally achieving up to 602.50x speedup.
The experimental results show that our method outperforms state of the art by scaling up to larger models with up to 138 million trainable parameters.

node_1: relu-based fully connected feedforward neural networks
edge: Verification problem for the simplest ReLU-based fully connected feedforward neural networks is NP-complete.
The proposed abstraction-based approach is for training verified robust image classifications.
Each pixel of an image is abstracted into an interval before inputting it into the neural network.
The interval is input to the neural network by assigning its lower and upper bounds to two input neurons.
Perturbations to the pixel in the interval do not alter the classification results.
Forward propagation and back propagation are used to train the network without extra time overhead.
Neural networks are treated as black boxes since only the input layer is dealt with and other layers are not changed.
The proposed approach is agnostic to the actual neural network architectures, allowing it to scale up to large neural networks.
A crucial hyper-parameter identified is abstraction granularity.
Abstraction granularity is a crucial hyper-parameter for training neural networks, where it corresponds to the size of intervals used for training the networks.
A gradient descent-based algorithm is proposed to refine the abstraction granularity for training robust neural networks.
The gradient descent-based algorithm is used to refine the abstraction granularity for training robust neural networks.
The method is implemented in a tool called ABSCERT.
The method reduces the verified errors of trained neural networks by up to 95.64%.
The method achieves up to 602.50x speedup.
A companion black-box verification method is introduced for certifying the robustness of trained neural networks.

Huber introduces the concept of robustness in deep neural networks where the efficiency of the model should be reasonably good and small disturbances applied to the input should have slight impact on the result.

Robustness verification aims to guarantee that a neural network outputs consistent classification results for all inputs in a set, which is usually represented as an lp norm ball around the clean image x
Many approaches rely on symbolic interval bound propagation to simplify the problem at the price of sacrificing completeness
When we know the domain of each input, we can estimate the output range by propagating the inputs symbolically throughout the neural network to prove/disprove the robustness
To accelerate the propagation, the non-linear activation functions need to over-approximate using linear constraints, therefore rendering the output range overestimated
More hidden layers in a network imply a larger overestimation because the overestimation is accumulated layer by layer
overestimation easily causes failure to verification

Function ϕ is used to transform the input (B(x, ϵ)) to interval vectors (I1, I2, . . . In) which are then fed into the image classifier f′.
Image classifier f is composed of f′ and ϕ, implying that it incorporates the classification results obtained from f′.
Training intervals are fed into the neural network by doubling the number of neurons in the input layer, with each neuron corresponding to the upper and lower bounds of a training interval.
Perturbation intervals are abstracted to training intervals using the function ϕ introduced in the abstraction process.
Abstraction granularity is represented by a constant d used to define the size of training interval vectors in the abstraction process.
Training on numerical intervals is done to train neural networks, where n denotes the size of interval vectors.
Norm ball B(x, ϵ) represents essentially an interval vector in In, and perturbation interval vectors are elements of In.
Complete input space range [-1, 1] is divided evenly into sub-intervals of size di to create the set of all sub-intervals Ii.
Training interval vectors are abstracted and mapped onto a finite number, which helps in robustness verification by restricting the perturbation space.
The purpose of abstraction is to ease the follow-up robustness verification by restricting the infinite perturbation space to finite training space.
The perturbation interval can only be mapped to a unique training interval, following certain constraints on the mapping process.
The abstraction granularity d is greater than or equal to twice the perturbation size to guarantee at most two training intervals intersect with a perturbation interval.
The perturbation interval is mapped to the training interval based on different cases like containment, unequal intersection, or equal intersection.
The perturbation interval is mapped to the training interval if it contains it. It may intersect with two training intervals at the same time.

If the size of the intersection of the perturbation interval and the numerically larger training interval is greater than or equal to the size of the intersection with the numerically smaller training interval, then the perturbation interval will be mapped to the numerically larger training interval.

If the size of the intersection of the perturbation interval and the numerically larger training interval is smaller than the size of the intersection with the numerically smaller training interval, then the perturbation interval will be mapped to the numerically smaller training interval.
The abstraction process results in a smaller variance of training intervals, demonstrating the effect of abstraction on input.

Many approaches on training neural networks with robustness guarantees have been proposed
Linear relaxation-based methods use linear relaxation to obtain a convex outer approximation within a norm-bounded perturbation. In contrast, bound propagation methods are more effective.
Gowal et al. proposed IBP to train provably robust neural networks on a relatively large scale.
Zhang et al. improved IBP by combining the fast IBP bounds in a forward propagation and a tight linear relaxation-based bound, CROWN, in a backward propagation.
Lee et al. proposed a training method based on linear approximation but considered another important factor - the smoothness of the loss function.
AdvIBP computes the adversarial loss using FGSM and random initialization.
AdvIBP computes the adversarial loss using FGSM and random initialization and computes the robust loss using IBP. However, the verified errors obtained by AdvIBP are relatively higher.
